g.data-narration: Welcome to this module on Characterizing Schedules Based on Recoverability in database management systems.
A schedule is a chronological sequence of operations from multiple concurrent transactions in a DBMS.
g.text: A schedule is a chronological sequence of read and write operations from
multiple concurrent transactions. It represents the execution order across all transactions.

g.data-narration: Recoverability ensures database consistency when transactions abort, preventing cascading rollbacks and data corruption.
g.text: When T2 reads values written by T1, and T1 later aborts, T2 has invalid data.
Recoverability prevents committed transactions from using uncommitted (possibly invalid) data.

g.data-narration: A recoverable schedule requires that if T_j reads data written by T_i, then T_i must commit before T_j commits.
g.text: Definition: If T_j reads a data item written by T_i, then Commit(T_i) must
occur before Commit(T_j). Transaction commits only after all its read-sources commit.

g.data-narration: In this recoverable schedule, T1 commits before T2 commits, ensuring T2 reads only committed data.
g.text: T1 commits before T2 reads A

g.data-narration: This schedule is non-recoverable because T2 commits after reading T1's uncommitted data, but T1 later aborts.
Cascadeless schedules are stricter: T_j must read only committed values from T_i, preventing cascading aborts.
g.text: Definition: Every transaction reads only committed values from other transactions.
Prevents cascading aborts where one abort forces other transactions to abort.
∀read by T_j of W_i: Commit(T_i) < Read(T_j)

g.data-narration: This schedule is cascadeless because T2 reads A only after T1 has committed, preventing cascading aborts.
g.text: T2 reads committed value of A

g.data-narration: This schedule causes cascading aborts because T2 reads T1's uncommitted data, forcing T2 to abort if T1 aborts.
Strict schedules provide highest recoverability: no transaction can read or write data from uncommitted transactions.
g.text: Definition: For every read/write by T_j on data written by T_i, Commit(T_i)
must occur before that operation. Highest level of recoverability.
∀op by T_j on W_i(X): Commit(T_i) < Op(T_j)

g.data-narration: This schedule is strict because T2 reads and writes A only after T1 has committed, ensuring full isolation.
This schedule is not strict because T2 reads A before T1 commits, violating the strict condition for reads.
The hierarchy shows Strict is the most restrictive, followed by Cascadeless, then Recoverable schedules.
Use these quick tests: Recoverable checks commit order, Cascadeless checks read timing, Strict checks all operations.
Key takeaways: Recoverability ensures consistency, use the hierarchy ST ⊂ ACA ⊂ RC, and always check commit-read order.
g.text: • Reading your own writes is always OK
• Strict = Most restrictive, RC = Least restrictive
